coredata.dc:title: Influence of phyto-derived nitrogen doped carbon dots from the seeds of Azadirachta indica on the NaBH4 reduction of Safranin-O dye
coredata.dc:creator: Dhanush, C.
Sethuraman, M.G.
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doped biogenic carbon dots have become promising candidates for diverse applications in the field of environment and medicine. In the present work, an eco-friendly and hydrothermal route was employed to synthesize highly fluorescent phyto derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (PDNCDs) from neem seeds. The prepared PDNCDs exhibited high water dispersity, excellent optical and fluorescent properties. The size of the PDNCDs was about 2.5 nm as given by the HR-TEM results. The PDNCDs were demonstrated to have high stability through zeta potential and TGA measurements. Their amorphous nature was explained from the results of XRD. The XPS, FT-IR and EDS measurements correlated well with each other in the characterization of functional moieties and elemental proportions of the PDNCDs. Raman spectroscopic and SAED results confess the graphitic nature of the PDNCDs. Studies with different solvents indicated that the properties of the PDNCDs can be fine-tuned. Finally, PDNCDs were found to have profound influence as catalyst in the NaBH4 reduction of Safranin-O, a phenazine dye, with the reduction completed within 6 min. The catalytic influence of PDNCDs could be elucidated on the basis of Langmuir- Hinshelwood mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neem seeds
Nitrogen doped CDs
Hydrothermal carbonization
Catalytic activity
Safranin-O dye

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of phyto-derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (PDNCDs)
Evaluation of the catalytic efficiency of PDNCDs
Morphology and surface state characterization of PDNCDs
Catalytic efficiency of the PDNCDs in the reduction of Safranin-O (SO) dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis absorption spectra of PDNCDs and the neem seed extract.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the PDNCDs at different excitations from 270 nm–320 nm, (b) Fluorescence spectra of the PDNCDs at different excitations from 320 nm–400 nm, (c) Fluorescence excitation spectrum of the PDNCDs.
(a) HR-TEM image of PDNCDs, (b) HR-TEM image of PDNCDs at 2 nm scale, (c) Particle distribution graph, (d) SAED pattern of the PDNCDs.
Zeta potential distribution of the PDNCDs.
FT-IR spectra of neem seed extract and the PDNCDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the PDNCDs. Deconvoluted HR-XPS spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s.
Influence of solvents on the λ max of the PDNCDs.

(a) Structure of SO dye, (b) Absorption spectra of SO dye, (c) Degradation absorption spectra of SO dye with NaBH4 (without PDNCDs), (d) Degradation absorption spectra of SO dye with NaBH4 (with PDNCDs).
Percentage (%) Degradation of SO dye (a) Without PDNCDs, (b) With PDNCDs.

(a) Kinetics plot of the SO dye degradation in the presence of PDNCDs (green catalyst). (b) Degradation absorption spectra of SO dye with NaBH4 (with undoped CDs). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Influence of phyto-derived nitrogen doped carbon dots from the seeds of Azadirachta indica on the NaBH4 reduction of Safranin-O dye

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University, Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute- Deemed to be University, Gandhigram- 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doped biogenic carbon dots have become promising candidates for diverse applications in the field of environment and medicine. In the present work, an eco-friendly and hydrothermal route was employed to synthesize highly fluorescent phyto derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (PDNCDs) from neem seeds. The prepared PDNCDs exhibited high water dispersity, excellent optical and fluorescent properties. The size of the PDNCDs was about 2.5 nm as given by the HR-TEM results. The PDNCDs were demonstrated to have high stability through zeta potential and TGA measurements. Their amorphous nature was explained from the results of XRD. The XPS, FT-IR and EDS measurements correlated well with each other in the characterization of functional moieties and elemental proportions of the PDNCDs. Raman spectroscopic and SAED results confess the graphitic nature of the PDNCDs. Studies with different solvents indicated that the properties of the PDNCDs can be fine-tuned. Finally, PDNCDs were found to have profound influence as catalyst in the NaBH4 reduction of Safranin-O, a phenazine dye, with the reduction completed within 6 min. The catalytic influence of PDNCDs could be elucidated on the basis of Langmuir- Hinshelwood mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the class of highly fluorescent and biocompatible carbon nanomaterials with the dimensions ranging from 1 to 10 nm. In 2004, during the refining of single wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) the serendipitous discovery of this new kind of carbon material was made [1]. Earlier, semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) had drawn the attention of the researchers due to their magnificent electronic and luminescent properties. But, the preparative methods of SQDs usually involve heavy metals and hence they are unfriendly to human beings as well as to the environment which restricted the applications of SQDs in the field of biomedicine and other biological applications [2]. Due to their low toxicity, photostability, tunable excitation dependent emission, high water solubility and chemical inactiveness, CDs have emerged as obvious alternatives to SQDs [3]. In fact, their applications play a major role in the fields of bio-imaging, sensing, drug delivery, optoelectronics and catalysis [1,4–8].

The preparative methods of CDs were categorized into two types viz., bottom-up and top-down. Methods like laser ablation, arc discharge, ultrasonic exfoliation, electrochemical methods and acid oxidizing exfoliation fall into the category of top-down approach. Hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis, micro-reactor and microwave assisted synthesis fall into the category of bottom-up approach [9]. Among these, arc discharge and laser ablation technique require expensive energy consuming equipments. Electrochemical methods, on the other hand, involve the usage of strong corrosive acids, while in the case of microwave irradiation, controlling the reaction conditions is not possible as desired. Hence, hydrothermal carbonization is the preferred method for the preparation of CDs. Doping of single and multiple hetero atoms into the CDs influences and enhances the electronic and optical properties [10].

Neem, belongs to Meliaceae family and it is biologically known as Azadirachta indica. It contains phytoconstituents like alkaloids, flavonoids, tannins, saponins etc., [11]. The medicinal use of neem in the treatment of infectious and metabolic diseases is well documented. Neem finds use as an insecticide too. It also exhibits multiple activities like anti-cancer, anti-viral, anti-fungal etc. Neem is abundantly found in tropical and semitropical regions like India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and Nepal [12].

Safranin-O (SO dye) is a phenazine dye and it is used as a photosensitizer in electron- and energy-transfer reactions. 3,7-diamino-2,8-dimethyl-5 phenyl phenazinium chloride is the chemical name of safranin-O dye [13]. The environmental and human health issues caused by the dyes and related compounds are well known. Some of the harmful effects produced are skin cancer, respiratory, pneumatic, and cardiovascular diseases as well as genetic disorders [14]. Physical, chemical and biological methods such as coagulation, flocculation, adsorption using activated carbon and reverse osmosis have been adopted to remove dyes from the effluents. Out of these methods, degradation process is one of the most common and fast deactivation methods available for the removal of dyes [14,15].

In this work, fluorescent phyto-derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (PDNCDs) were synthesized from the seeds of Azadirachta indica. For the source of carbon, neem seeds were used as the precursors and aqueous NH3 was used as the nitrogen source (dopant). Optical characterizations, surface morphological studies and structural analysis were done for the PDNCDs. Finally, their utility in the reduction of the Safranin-O dye as catalyst with NaBH4 was explored.

Ripened Azadirachta indica fruits were freshly handpicked from the campus of Gandhigram Rural Institute. Aqueous NH3 purchased from CDH chemicals, NaBH4 and Safranin-O dye purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Milli Q water was used to experiment the work.

After rinsing the fruit thoroughly, yellow colored exocarp and the pulp of the fruit were dissected out from the seed embedded with it. With a domestic mixer grinder, the seeds were crushed down with water. The aqueous solution of the seed extract was collected, filtered and the clear extract of the neem seed was used as the source of carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phyto-derived nitrogen doped carbon dots (PDNCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Simple hydrothermal carbonization method was implemented to synthesize nitrogen doped carbon dots (PDNCDs). In this method, to a 100 ml teflon lined cylinder, neem seed extract (55 ml) and aqueous NH3 (5 ml) were added in respective proportion and placed into an autoclave. Then the tightly closed autoclave was placed in a hot air oven and maintained at 180 °C for about 12 h. After the fulfillment of the reaction time, the stainless steel autoclave was set free to reach the room temperature. The resultant dark brown colored PDNCDs were filtered with Whatman-40 filter paper and then centrifuged to remove the unwanted bulk materials. Finally, the PDNCDs were stored in a refrigerator at 5 °C for future property assessments.

Optical properties like UV-VIS absorbance and photoluminescence behavior of the PDNCDs were assessed using JASCO V-750 spectrophotometer and JASCO SP 8500 spectrofluorometer respectively. The size and framework of the PDNCDs were characterized using FEI- TECNAI T20 G2 HR-TEM instrument in which the dilute solution of the PDNCDs were placed on the copper grids and dried at normal room temperature and then analyzed. In PDNCDs, the functional groups present were characterized using Jasco-FT-I460 plus spectrophotometer by KBr pellet technique. XRD patterns were recorded using the PANalytical X'pert diffractometer. EDS data was obtained using BRUKER Nano, GmBh, D-12489 (Germany) (Accelerating voltage 0–30 KeV) instrument. To obtain Raman spectra, STR 500 nm focal length laser Raman spectrometer was utilized. To evaluate the chemical proportions of the PDNCDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) technique was used for which PHI 5000 Versa Prob II,FEI Inc. instrument was used at a scan rate of 30 eV for half an hour. Thermal measurements were done using NETZSCH STA-2500 Regulus TGA instrument. Zetasizer nano-zs90 malvern instrument was used for zeta potential measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the catalytic efficiency of PDNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the catalytic efficiency of the PDNCDs, reduction of SO dye using NaBH4 experiment was performed with and without the PDNCDs. Several trails were carried out to optimize the concentrations of both the dye and NaBH4. About 1 ml of SO dye (1 × 10−3 M), 0.5 ml of NaBH4 (0.25 M) (fresh) were taken in glass cuvette with 1 cm path length and was diluted with 1.5 ml of water. In an another subsequent reaction, to the above combination 10 μl of the PDNCDs was added. Reduction was monitored spectroscopically by mapping the absorbance value at 520 nm at periodic intervals.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the neem seed extract and the PDNCDs are shown in the Fig. 1 . The molecules containing bonding and non-bonding electrons which can absorb energy in the form of ultraviolet or visible light to excite these electrons to higher anti-bonding molecular orbitals can be characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Aqueous seed extract showed a shoulder at 290 nm and the highly dispersed PDNCDs in aqueous solution depicted two absorption maxima, one at 270 nm which explains the π-π* transition of the CC group and the another at 306 nm which explains the n-π* transition of the CO/CN moieties of the PDNCDs. Lic et al., reported the same characteristic feature of the shoulders obtained for their nitrogen doped carbon dots material [16]. Upon addition of aqueous NH3, there was an appreciable change in the absorption maxima of the neem seed extract (290 nm). The phytochemical constituents of the neem seed extract include flavonoids, which are polyphenols. It is likely that upon addition of aqueous NH3, the transformation of these phenolics to ammonium phenolate takes place [17].

Fluorescence spectroscopy is a complementary to UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. The fluorescence study was carried out at a wavelength interval of 10 nm. PDNCDs exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. 2a) at about 400 nm when excited at 320 nm excitation. There was no characteristic shift in the wavelength till the excitation was varied from 270 to 320 nm and it was attributed to the π*-π transitions of the cores of graphitic carbon. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 nm to 400 nm, there was a progressive decrease in the fluorescence intensity and correspondingly there was a bathochromic shift (red shift) in the excitation wavelength which was due to the radiative recombination of the π*-n transitions (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c depicts the excitation spectrum of the PDNCDs. One of the most enchanting properties of PDNCDs was excitation-dependent emission and this was because of variation in sizes, different surface states, nature of surface defects and also due to the presence of different organic functional moieties like -OH, -NH2 and COOH on the PDNCDs.

Atchudan et al., prepared the nitrogen doped CDs from the unripe fruit extract of Prunus mume, where their fluorescent features aligns to be a proof of the fluorescent behavior of PDNCDs. Roy et al., explained the mechanism of fluorescence emission, and these reports add a proof to the fluorescent emission and excitation property of the PDNCDs [18,19]. The ability of nitrogen atom to donate electrons to CDs could result in the change of the internal electronic environment and may lead to the enhancement of the fluorescent properties of the PDNCDs [20]. Wide range of fluorescent spectra exhibited by PDNCDs could be the reason for CDs being used in bio-imaging and fluorescent biological applications [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface state characterization of PDNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: HR-TEM images revealed the size and morphological nature of the PDNCDs (Fig. 3a). PDNCDs were spherical in shape with 2.5 nm size and their diameter ranges from 1.2 nm to 5 nm. Lattice fringes of the PDNCDs are shown in the Fig. 3b and it corresponds to the phase of the graphitic carbon (002) and (100) [22]. In PDNCDs, the amorphous nature was also signified by the presence of diffused disk pattern of the SAED. Chandrasekaran et al., demonstrated the amorphous nature of the carbon dots in which their results matches with the PDNCDs [23].
EDS spectrum of the PDNCDs is presented in Fig. 4 . The PDNCDs contained elements such as C (63.61%), O (20.81%) and N (15.58%).

The surface state of a nanoparticle and their long period stability can be identified with the help of zeta potential values. Nanoparticles have high stability when their zeta potential values are greater than +25 mV or less than −25 mV. Nanoparticles having less zeta potential values will ultimately aggregate due to inter particle Van der Waals attractions [24]. The zeta potential distribution of PDNCDs (Fig. 5 ) was found to be −20.4 mV which vouched for their higher stability. The observed negative value may be attributed to the presence of carbonyl, hydroxyl and amine functional groups present on the surface of PDNCDs and it is also confirmed by the FT-IR results. Similarly, when nitrogen is doped it enhances the electronic nature of the PDNCDs to the maximum. These carbonyl and hydroxyl groups are the major reasons for the hydrophilic nature of the PDNCDs and also for their excellent water solubility. Wang et al., had attributed the zeta potential value of −20.6 mV for the nitrogen doped CDs obtained from milk to the presence of hydroxy and carboxylic groups. The zeta potential in the present work was measured to be −20.4 mV which could also be correlated to the presence of functional groups such as OH and COOH groups of PDNCDS [25].

The XRD pattern of PDNCDs is displayed in the Fig. 6 . The 2θ values appeared at 28.470 (broad peak) and 40.60 (weak peak), correspond to (002) and (100) lattice planes respectively. The broad diffraction peak evidences that the PDNCDs are amorphous with closely packed carbon atoms. Using Bragg's equation the d-spacing value was calculated for the PDNCDs. For the broad and weak peak the d-spacing values were 0.313 nm and 0.221 nm. Wei et al. reported the 2θ and the d-spacing values of the CDs obtained from tea leaves as 26.4 and 0.30 nm respectively, and confirmed the amorphous nature of the CDs. XRD results of the present work agrees well with the reports of Wei et al., and the amorphous nature of the PDNCDs is also confirmed [26].

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 7 ) establishes the various functional moieties present in the neem seed extract and the PDNCDs. The neem seed extract showed absorption bands at 3432 cm−1, 2926 cm−1, 1732 cm−1 and 1210 cm−1 due to the presence of OH, CH, CO and COC functional moieties accordingly. The absorption bands 3406 cm−1, 2922 cm−1, 1700 cm−1, 1601 cm−1, 1389 cm−1 and 1165 cm−1 correspond to the presence of OH/NH, CH, CO, CC, COC and CN functional moieties respectively. The stretching frequency due to OH group got shifted from 3432 cm−1 to 3406 cm−1. There was also a shift from 2926 cm−1 to 2922 cm−1 in the case of CH functional moiety when compared with the FT-IR bands of both the neem seed extract and the PDNCDs. Thus the FT-IR spectra revealed the presence of acid, carbonyl, amine and hydroxyl functional moieties. FT-IR spectra and data reveal the conformation of the functional group and corresponding wavenumbers which was already reported by Amin et al. [27,28]. The functional groups in the PDNCDs could well be the reason for enhancing the solubility of the PDNCDs in aqueous medium.

Raman spectroscopy serves as a useful tool to characterize the carbon based nanomaterials. The Raman spectra of the PDNCDs (Fig. 8 ) displayed two distinctive bands at 1369 cm−1 (D band-defects of sp3-hybridized graphitic carbon due to the dangling bonds in the termination plane of the amorphous structure) and 1606 cm−1 (G band-vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon) respectively. The ratio of the peak intensities of both the bands (ID/IG) of the synthesized PDNCDs was 0.79 and this value evidenced that the PDNCDs were amorphous. He et al., reported the D and G band values (1344 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1) of the CDs obtained from citric acid. It showed the disordered sp3 and crystalline sp2 nature of the prepared CDs. The lower graphitic nature of the CDs was explained on the basis of ID/IG ratio (0.85) [28,29]. Thus, the reported data of He et al., align well with the results of the present work.

Thermal stability of the PDNCDs was assessed by the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Fig. 9 shows the TGA curve of the PDNCDs at nitrogen atmosphere. Weight loss was continuously observed up to 600 °C. Initially, there was a 10% weight loss due to the desorption of the water molecules which were physically adsorbed on the PDNCDs. Subsequent weight loss was obtained from 300 to 400 °C, which was due to the decomposition of the oxygen possessing groups. When temperature was increased up to 600 °C, there was 60% of weight loss which could be traced to the decomposition of functional groups and the carbon skeleton of PDNCDs [30].

The surface distribution/functionalization and elemental states of the PDNCDs were evaluated by XPS. Full survey scan spectrum of the PDNCDs (Fig. 10a) showed three distinct peaks at 282.4, 396.8 and 528.8 eV indicating the existence of carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s) and oxygen (O1s) elements respectively. Fig. 10b displays three distinct peaks of C1s which appeared at 284.8, 286.85 and 292.5 eV due to the presence of CH/CC (sp2), CN/CO and π electron delocalization (satellite peak) respectively [31–33]. The C1s peak at 284.8 eV shows the existence of sp2 carbon atoms on the PDNCDs [34]. Fig. 10c shows the deconvoluted N1s peaks at 398.35, 399.4 and 400.65 eV indicating the existence of CNC/CNH2, NH/OCNH2 and pyridinic nitrogen on the PDNCDs [28,35]. Fig. 10d displays the deconvoluted O1s peaks at 530.7, 531.8 and 532.7 eV confirming the existence of COH, CO/CNO and COC moieties [2,36,37]. Thus the data from the XPS clearly showed the existence of C (75.8%), N (11.85%) and O (12.35%) in the PDNCDs confirming the doping of nitrogen on the PDNCDs. These results from XPS support the results of FT-IR and EDS spectra. Pacquiao et al., reported the values of C1s peaks, to be 284.9, 286.3, and 288.2 eV which correspond to CC, CO, and CO of carboxyl groups [31]. Liu et al., reported the values of N1s peaks, to be 398.4, 399 and 399.8 eV which correspond to CNC, N(C)3, pyridinic-like N and pyrrolic-like N [32] and Jing et al., reported the values of O1s peaks, to be 530.2, 530.9 and 531.6 eV which correspond to COH, CO and COC respectively [36]. Thus, the results of the present work tally with the above reported data.

Solvent dependent effect is an easy and methodical route to regulate the optical properties of the CDs. Solvents influence the wavelength of absorption of the PDNCDs. Complex interactions occur between the surface groups and solvent molecules when the PDNCDs were dissolved in solvents. A vital influence on the optical properties of the PDNCDs was exerted by this solvent effect [38,39]. In this study, the influence of solvents such as (water, ethanol, DMSO, acetone, toluene) were determined by measuring the absorbance of PDNCDs (0.5 ml) in 2.5 ml of the solvent. In the displayed absorbance spectrum, two absorption maxima appeared for each solvent which attributes to the high energy core band shift (π-π*) and lower energy core band shift (n-π*). As the polarity of the solvents increased, there was a blue shift in the high energy core band (from 278 nm to 270 nm) and also in the lower energy core band (from 326 nm to 304 nm) except toluene. When toluene was used as the solvent, inconsistent spectra was obtained due to solubility issues and aggregation of the PDNCDs. As the polarity of the solvent increased the absorption maxima was shifted towards the lower wavelength (hypsochromic shift) [40]. In case of protic solvents, the solvent dependent properties of the PDNCDs could be attributed to the hydrogen bond interactions between the protic solvents and PDNCDs [41]. In case of aprotic solvents, as the polarity of the solvents increased the surface electronic structure was highly influenced by the dipole moment which leads to the reduction of the energy gap and shift of the peak position towards longer wavelength [38]. Thus it is clear that optical properties of the PDNCDs could be fine-tuned by the change of solvent (See Fig. 11 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic efficiency of the PDNCDs in the reduction of Safranin-O (SO) dye
ce:section.ce:para: CDs act as catalytic agents. Reduction of SO dye with NaBH4 in the presence and absence of the PDNCDs (green catalyst) was experimented in this work. SO dye showed an absorption maxima at 520 nm in water medium (characteristic peak) as given in Fig. 12(b). The reduction of SO dye was undertaken by the addition of NaBH4 and the reaction was observed using UV–Visible spectrophotometer by measuring the absorbance at 520 nm at regular time intervals. There was a progressive decrease in the absorption intensity of the SO dye after the addition of NaBH4 when monitored at regular time intervals. In the absence of PDNCDs, even after 50 min the reduction of SO dye was comparatively much less (Fig. 12c). When the reaction was carried out with undoped CDs, it took nearly 28 min for degradation (Fig. 14b), but when the same reaction was carried out for the second time by adding 10 μl (0.2 wt/vol%) of the synthesized PDNCDs (green catalyst), complete reduction took place within 6 min (Fig. 12d). This showed the influence of CDs on the reduction of SO dye by NaBH4.

The mechanism of the SO dye degradation is precisely explained by the Langmuir- Hinshelwood mechanism [28]. SO dye and the BH4 − ions were adsorbed on the surface of the PDNCDs. These PDNCDs act as a surface mediator for the transfer of electrons from the BH4 − ions to the SO dye. Due to this surface intermediator role of PDNCDs, the energy boundary between the reactant and the product was reduced [42,43] and the degradation reaction was completed within a span of 6 min with evolution of H2 gas. The excellent catalytic efficiency of the PDNCDs could well be due to the presence of large surface area and high electron intake capability of the PDNCDs. The percentage degradation of the dye was established with the help of following equation,
% of degradation =
A 0
−
A t
/
A 0
× 100
[where, A0 – Initial absorbance; At – Absorbance at time (t)].

A straight line graph was obtained in the plot of Time (min) Vs % of degradation. This indicated the sequential degradation of the SO-dye. As evident from the graph, 50% degradation of SO dye could only be achieved by 50 min when PDNCDs were not present, while with PDNCDs, the extent of degradation was up to 95% that too within 6 min (Fig. 13 a & b). In addition to that, rate constant calculations were also done. Rate constant (k) for the degradation of the SO dye was evaluated using the pseudo-first order kinetics equation
ln
A t
/
A 0
= − kt
.
where, A0 and At are the absorbance values at zero and definite time. In the Fig. 14a, a graph was plot between time and (ln At/A0) in the respective X and Y coordinates. The graph displayed a good linearity, where, k and coefficient determination (R2) were obtained from the slope of the linear plot. The corresponding k and R2 values calculated from the linear graph are0.13283 min−1 and 0.99226. But, in the absence of PDNCDs the linearity between (ln At/A0) and time was not obtained. Thus, the study highlighted the potential of the PDNCDs as a green catalyst on the NaBH4 degradation of the SO dye.

Thus it is evident that nitrogen doped fluorescent carbon dots of 2.5 nm size could be synthesized using the seeds of neem as carbon precursor. Optical properties of the PDNCDs were explained from the results of UV–Vis and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The PDNCDs were found to be highly graphitic and had functional groups like amine, acid, carbonyl and hydroxyl moieties. As evidenced by the zeta potential values, the prepared PDNCDs had high stability. Studies with various solvents revealed the possibility to fine tune the optical properties. These PDNCDs were established to have profound influence as catalyst in the reduction of SO-dye by NaBH4. The catalytic effect of the PDNCDs could be explained based on the ability to mediate electron transfer from BH4 − ions to the dye and also with the help of kinetics data. Deployment of PDNCDs in the detoxification of effluents with dyes such as SO could well become the robust strategy in the removal of organic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank for the financial support given by the Department of Science and Technology - Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB) for this work through a research project on “Eco-friendly Catalysis” (File No: EMR/2016/006024).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotubes fragments
Journal of American Chemical Society
